title: Pituitary stalk lesion -> high prolactin
vignette: 29-year-old with sarcoidosis, headaches, bitemporal hemianopia; MRI shows a mass involving the hypothalamus and pituitary stalk. Which pituitary hormone is most likely elevated?

clues.note: Stalk carries dopamine that tonically inhibits prolactin.
Optic chiasm compression by a sellar/suprasellar mass.

whatMatters: Prolactin is the only anterior pituitary hormone under tonic INHIBITION (dopamine); cut the stalk -> prolactin rises (stalk effect). All others fall.
strategy: Stalk lesion: releasing-hormone-dependent hormones drop; the inhibited one (prolactin) rises. ADH falls -> central DI.

eliminations.why: Stalk loss -> ACTH falls (central adrenal insufficiency).
eliminations.correctIf: Question asked which anterior pituitary hormone UNIQUELY RISES with stalk section (only prolactin does)
Same as ACTH — GH decreases with stalk section due to loss of GHRH
Question asked about the secondary hypothyroidism that develops from stalk damage (TSH falls)
Question asked which condition develops from stalk damage (central DI from ADH deficiency is also correct)

reasoning: Sarcoid mass on hypothalamus + pituitary stalk
Stalk carries dopamine that inhibits prolactin
mechanism: The pituitary stalk conveys hypothalamic control. Prolactin is uniquely under tonic dopamine inhibition; a stalk lesion blocks dopamine -> hyperprolactinemia. Differential: TSH-secreting adenoma (up alpha-subunit, MRI).

keywords.front: Only anterior pituitary hormone tonically inhibited

memoryHook: Cut the stalk -> only prolactin climbs.
